title: Electric current
paperName: Paper 1: Multiple Choice / Paper 2: AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Free premium 9702 lesson on electric current — charge flow, I = Q/t, conventional current, and how Cambridge examiners award marks on circuit questions.
learningObjectives: Define electric current and relate it to charge flow per unit time
Distinguish conventional current direction from electron flow
Apply I = Q/t in structured and MCQ questions
Read ammeter placement and units correctly under exam conditions

simpleExplanation.title: Electric current — like water in a pipe
simpleExplanation.summary: Current is how much charge passes a point each second. Imagine coulombs of charge as drops of water — current tells you the flow rate.
simpleExplanation.analogy: A wider pipe (more charge per second) means higher current, just as a wider hose carries more litres per minute.
steps: Conventional current flows from + to − (opposite to electrons)
Always state units: A (ampere), Q (coulomb), t (second)
Practise one past-paper circuit question and mark it

sections.content: **Electric current** is the rate of flow of charge. Almost every **9702** electricity question assumes you are fluent with **I = Q/t**, units, and the direction convention examiners use in mark schemes.
I = Q / t   where I is current (A), Q is charge (C), t is time (s)
One ampere equals one coulomb per second. In exams, quote the definition before substituting numbers — definition marks are easy if you write them cleanly.

items: Conventional current: positive terminal → negative terminal
Electrons flow the opposite way — say so if asked about particle flow
Ammeters measure current; connect in series
Charge may be given in mC — convert to C before substituting
Constant current ⇒ Q–t graph is a straight line through origin

sections.question: A current of 2.5 A flows in a wire for 4.0 minutes. Calculate the charge that passes a point in the wire.

sections.content: Paper 2 often combines current with resistance and potential difference in one circuit. Master I = Q/t first so you do not lose early marks before reaching V = IR.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on electric current

faq.q: Is this 9702 electric current lesson free?
faq.a: Yes — the full Cambridge 9702 course on MarkScheme is free, including premium lessons with past-paper questions and mark-scheme marking.

faq.q: What past papers test electric current (9.1)?
faq.a: Current appears on almost every electricity MCQ and structured paper. Use the past-paper section on this page for real Cambridge questions from our library.

faq.q: How is this different from ZNotes?
faq.a: We teach syllabus point 9.1 with worked examples, a simpler explanation mode, and real past-paper practice with AI marking against official schemes — not static PDF notes alone.
